summary: Drew DeVault argues that Signal has failed its users by introducing cryptocurrency and maintaining centralized control, and lays out requirements for what the next chat application should look like. He insists it must be a federated protocol (not just an app), with end-to-end encryption, user anonymity, and a focus on reliability over feature bloat. The post is a direct response to Signal's crypto announcement, which DeVault sees as validation of his longstanding concerns about centralized governance. He calls for a system that distributes power across independent server operators and resists the temptation to cargo-cult toxic features from existing chat apps.
key_points: Signal's introduction of cryptocurrency validates concerns about centralized control and governance in chat platforms
A chat system should be a federated protocol with multiple client/server implementations, not a single proprietary app
End-to-end encryption is essential but enormously complex, especially combined with federation, group chats, and multi-device support
Users should have anonymity by default — no phone numbers, minimal data collection, with support for pseudonymous and multiple identities
Developers should focus on reliability and core messaging before adding features like typing notifications and read receipts, many of which are 'pretty toxic'
money_quotes: Signal has officially jumped the shark with the introduction of cryptocurrency to their chat app.

A chat app which has only one implementation and a private protocol can only ever meet the needs that its developers (1) foresee, (2) care about, and (3) have the capacity to address; thus, such a protocol cannot be ubiquitous.
We simply cannot trust a single entity, even you, dear reader, to have such a large degree of influence over the ecosystem.
I do not trust you not to add some crypto Ponzi scheme of your own 5 years from now.
Signal wasn't, and has had problems ranging from 502 Server Gone errors to 404 Ethics Not Found errors, both of which are solved by federation.
The most secure data is the data you don't have.
Typing notifications and read receipts are pretty toxic, if you examine them critically.
And don't put a fucking cryptocurrency in it.
key_insight: The next chat platform must be a federated, open protocol that distributes power across independent operators, because centralized control — no matter how well-intentioned — inevitably leads to abuse.
